root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

the algebraic theory IV: operations on sheaves of modules

Table of contents

  1. Étale spaces and sheafification
  2. Pullbacks
  3. Sheaf homs
  4. Tensor products
  5. Dual sheaves
  6. Tangent and cotangent sheaves
  7. Natural isomorphisms

Prerequisites

Sheaf homs

Definition. Let $(X,\sco_X)$ be a locally ringed space and $\cale$ and $\calf$ two $\sco_X$-modules. We define the sheaf hom (or internal hom), denoted $\ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)$, to be the $\sco_X$-module with sections over an open set $U\subseteq X$ given by

\begin{equation}\notag \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)(U) = \Hom_{\sco_X|_U}\left(\cale|_U,\calf|_U\right), \end{equation}

where the Hom on the right-hand side is the hom-set in the category $\Mod{\sco_X|_U}$. If $U\subseteq V$ is an inclusion of open sets, the restriction map

\begin{equation}\notag \rho^V_U: \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)(V) \to \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)(U) \end{equation}

is defined by $\rho^V_U(\eta)_W = \eta_W$ for each open set $W\subseteq U$.

Exercise.

  1. Prove that $\ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)$ is a sheaf of sets on $X$.
  2. We define the sum of two sections $\eta,\theta \in \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)(V)$, denoted $\eta + \theta$, to be the section with \begin{equation}\notag (\eta + \theta)_U(s) = \eta_U(s) + \theta_U(s) \end{equation} for all open sets $U\subseteq V$ and sections $s\in \cale(U)$. The sum on the right-hand side represents the addition in the $\sco_X(U)$-module $\calf(U)$. With respect to this addition, prove $\ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)$ is a sheaf of abelian groups.
  3. We define the product of a section $\ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)$ and a function $f\in \sco_X(V)$, denoted $f\eta$, to be the section with \begin{equation}\notag (f\eta)_U(s) = f|_U\eta_U(s) \end{equation} for all open sets $U\subseteq V$ and sections $s\in \cale(U)$. The product on the right-hand side represents the product in the $\sco_X(U)$-module $\calf(U)$. With respect to this product and the addition defined in the previous part, prove that $\ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)$ is an $\sco_X$-module.

Having carefully defined sheaf homs, we now study their stalks and fibers.

Theorem (Stalks of sheaf homs). Let $(X,\sco_X)$ be a locally ringed space and $\cale$ and $\calf$ two $\sco_X$-modules. For each $p\in X$, there is a natural $\sco_{X,p}$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag S_p: \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)_p \to \Hom_{\sco_{X,p}}(\cale_p,\calf_p) \end{equation}

induced by the map $\eta \mapsto \eta_p$ on sections. If $\cale$ is locally free, this map is an isomorphism.

I will sketch a proof that $S_p$ is an isomorphism when $\cale$ is locally free (and I will let you prove that $S_p$ is well-defined and $\sco_{X,p}$-linear). By replacing $X$ with an open neighborhood $U$ of $p$ over which $\cale$ trivializes, we may as well assume $\cale$ is (globally) free. An $\sco_X$-linear isomorphism

\begin{equation}\label{iso-eqn} \cale \xrightarrow{\cong} \sco_X^k \end{equation}

(for some $k$) induces an isomorphism

\begin{equation}\label{poop-eqn} \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\sco_X^k,\calf) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf) \end{equation}

of $\sco_X$-modules. But there is also a sequence of isomorphisms

\begin{equation}\label{seq-eqn} \calf^k \xrightarrow{\cong} \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\sco_X,\calf)^k \xrightarrow{\cong} \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\sco_X^k,\calf) \end{equation}

of $\sco_X$-modules. Linking this string of isomorphisms with \eqref{poop-eqn} and localizing at $p$ gives an $\sco_{X,p}$-linear isomorphism

\begin{equation}\label{iso1-eqn} \calf_p^k \xrightarrow{\cong} \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)_p \end{equation}

of $\sco_{X,p}$-modules. On the other hand, by localizing the isomorphism \eqref{iso-eqn} at $p$, we get an $\sco_{X,p}$-linear isomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag \cale_p \xrightarrow{\cong} \sco_{X,p}^k. \end{equation}

There are then versions of the isomorphisms \eqref{poop-eqn} and \eqref{seq-eqn} with the $\sco_X$-modules replaced with their stalks and the sheaf homs replaced with $\Hom_{\sco_{X,p}}$. Linking them together, we get an isomorphism

\begin{equation}\label{iso2-eqn} \calf_p^k \xrightarrow{\cong} \Hom_{\sco_{X,p}}(\cale_p,\calf_p). \end{equation}

Then, all that you need to show to complete the proof that $S_p$ is an isomorphism is that the isomorphism \eqref{iso2-eqn} factors through the isomorphism \eqref{iso1-eqn} and $S_p$. Q.E.D.

Having computed the stalks of sheaf homs, at least in the case that the first sheaf is locally free, we now turn to fibers. We first need a purely algebraic lemma:

Lemma. Let $\phi:A \to B$ be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let $M$ and $N$ be two $A$-modules. Then there is a natural $B$-linear homomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag \Hom_A(M,N) \otimes_A B \to \Hom_B(M \otimes_A B, N \otimes_A B), \quad \alpha \otimes g \mapsto \alpha \otimes \mu_g, \end{equation}

where $\mu_g:B \to B$ is the “multiplication by $g$” homomorphism. In particular, the map $\alpha \otimes \mu_g$ is defined on a tensor $s \otimes g’\in M \otimes_A B$ by

\begin{equation}\notag (\alpha \otimes \mu_g) (s \otimes g’) = \alpha(s) \otimes gg’. \end{equation}

If $M$ is free (of finite rank), then this map is an isomorphism.

Exercise. Prove the lemma!

As we now show, the natural map on stalks that we obtained in the previous theorem fits into a morphism that allows us to compute fibers of sheaf homs, at least in the case that the first sheaf is locally free:

Theorem (Fibers of sheaf homs). Let $(X,\sco_X)$ be a locally ringed space and $\cale$ and $\calf$ two $\sco_X$-modules. For each $p\in X$, there is a natural $\kappa(p)$-linear map

\begin{equation}\label{stair-eqn} \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)_p \otimes_{\sco_{X,p}} \kappa(p) \to \Hom_{\kappa(p)}(E_p, F_p) \end{equation}

obtained as the composition of the natural map

\begin{equation}\notag S_p \otimes \kappa(p): \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\calf)_p \otimes_{\sco_{X,p}} \kappa(p) \to \Hom_{\sco_{X,p}}(\cale_p,\calf_p) \otimes_{\sco_{X,p}}\kappa(p) \end{equation}

(where $S_p$ is the map from the previous theorem) along with the natural map

\begin{equation}\notag \Hom_{\sco_{X,p}}(\cale_p,\calf_p) \otimes_{\sco_{X,p}} \kappa(p) \to \Hom_{\kappa(p)}(E_p, F_p) \end{equation}

from the previous lemma. If $\cale$ is locally free, then \eqref{stair-eqn} is an isomorphism.

Exercise. Prove the theorem! Hint: Of course, essentially all of the hard work has already been completed in the previous theorem and lemma. Don’t work too hard!

Tensor products

Dual sheaves

Definition. Let $(X,\sco_X)$ be a locally ringed space and $\cale$ an $\sco_X$-module. We define the dual of $\cale$, denoted $\cale^\ast$, as the $\sco_X$-module

\begin{equation}\notag \cale^\ast = \ShHom_{\sco_X}(\cale,\sco_X). \end{equation}

Of course, if $\cale$ is a locally free $\sco_X$-module, then its dual $\cale^\ast$ is also locally free, of the same rank.

Definition. Let $(X,\sco_X)$ be a locally ringed space and $\cale$ an $\sco_X$-module. The contraction mapping is the $\sco_X$-linear morphism

\begin{equation}\notag \ang{-,-} : \cale \otimes_{\sco_X} \cale^\ast \to \sco_X, \quad s\otimes \eta \mapsto \ang{s,\eta} \defeq \eta(s). \end{equation}

Blah!

Definition. Let $(X,\sco_X)$ be a locally ringed space and $\cale$ a free $\sco_X$-module of finite rank. Bases $s_1,\ldots,s_k$ of $\cale$ and $\eta^1,\ldots,\eta^k$ of $\cale^\ast$ are said to be dually paired if

\begin{equation}\notag \ang{s_j,\eta^i} = \delta^i_j \defeq \begin{cases} 1 & : i=j, \\ 0 & : i\neq j,\end{cases} \end{equation}

for $1\leq i,j\leq k$.

Blah!

Theorem (Dually paired bases exist). Let $(X,\sco_X)$ be a locally ringed space and $\cale$ a free $\sco_X$-module of finite rank. Then for any basis $s_1,\ldots,s_k$ of $\cale$, a unique dually paired basis $\eta^1,\ldots,\eta^k$ of $\cale^\ast$ exists.

Cotangent sheaves

Definition. Let $(M,\sco_M)$ be a smooth manifold with tangent sheaf $\calx_M$. We define the cotangent sheaf of $M$, denoted $\calx_M^\ast$, to be the dual sheaf

\begin{equation}\notag \calx^\ast_M = \ShHom_{\sco_M}(\calx_M,\sco_M). \end{equation}

If $U\subseteq M$ is open, then, by definition, a section of the cotangent sheaf

\begin{equation}\notag \omega \in \calx^\ast_M(U) \end{equation}

is an $\sco_M|_U$-linear morphism

\begin{equation}\notag \omega: \calx_M|_U \to \sco_M|_U \end{equation}

of modules. So, for each open subset $V\subseteq U$, we have an $\sco_M(V)$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \omega_V : \calx_M(V) \to \sco_M(V) \end{equation}

which produces smooth functions over $V$ from smooth vector fields over $V$.

But we already know how to produce examples of these types of sections! For example, suppose that we are given a smooth function $f\in \sco_M(U)$. Then for each $p\in U$ we have the usual differential

\begin{equation}\notag df_p: T_p(M) \to \bbr. \end{equation}

In fact, for each open subset $V\subseteq U$, there is an induced $\sco_M(V)$-linear map

\begin{equation}\label{mirror-eqn} d_U(f)_V: \calx_M(V) \to \sco_M(V), \quad X \mapsto (p \mapsto df_p(X_p)). \end{equation}

To see that the differential $df_p(X_p)$ smoothly depends on $p$, we need only express it in local coordinates $x^1,\ldots,x^n$ near $p$ as

\begin{equation}\notag df_p(X_p) = X^i(p) \frac{\bd f}{\bd x^i}(p) \end{equation}

where $X^i$ are the (local) smooth component functions of $X$. But the right-hand side of this equation smoothly depends on $p$, and hence so does $df_p(X_p)$. This proves that the target of \eqref{mirror-eqn} really is the algebra of smooth functions $\sco_M(V)$. After you verify that $d_U(f)_V$ is $\sco_M(V)$-linear, we may conclude that $d_U(f)$ is a section in $\calx_M^\ast(U)$.

In fact, what we’ve produced is a function

\begin{equation}\label{post-eqn} d_U: \sco_M(U) \to \calx_M^\ast(U). \end{equation}

The next important theorem shows that these functions assemble together to yield a morphism of sheaves:

Theorem (Differentials as connections on structure sheaves). Let $(M,\sco_M)$ be a smooth manifold with cotangent sheaf $\calx_M^\ast$. Then the maps \eqref{post-eqn} define an $\underline{\bbr}_M$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag d: \sco_M \to \calx_M^\ast. \end{equation}

This map is a derivation, in the sense that for functions $f,g\in \sco_M(U)$ we have

\begin{equation}\notag d(fg) = f dg + (df)g. \end{equation}

That $d$ commutes with the restriction maps and is $\underline{\bbr}_M$-linear depends crucially on the fact that the differential is a local operation, in the sense that the value $df_p$ is unaffected by restriction of $f$ to any open neighborhood of $p$. Carefully go through the proof of the theorem on your own to see why.
